what_it_does: A research finding that tests the thesis of replacing vLLM's hand-guessed gpu_memory_utilization fraction with an absolute --kv-cache-memory-bytes budget and examines whether load order stops mattering, using source-line references and boot logs from vLLM 0.21.0.
observations: --kv-cache-memory-bytes exists in CacheConfig and, when set, makes vLLM return the specified bytes directly and skip the requested-minus-non_kv sizing subtraction.
The startup request_memory gate still runs unconditionally and still uses gpu_memory_utilization as a fraction of total GPU memory, so an absolute KV budget does not remove the startup gate.
profile_run() still executes even with kv_cache_memory_bytes set, so the activation-peak load instant still needs physical headroom.
The real crash-loop root cause was gpu_util=0.35 producing requested=5.60 GiB, which is below the model weights of 5.67 GiB, yielding negative KV before any co-tenant memory entered the math.
non_torch_increase is computed as a diff from a baseline snapshot taken after co-tenants are resident, so stable co-tenants cancel out; only churn during the profile window affects the budget.
VLLM_MEMORY_PROFILER_ESTIMATE_CUDAGRAPHS defaults to True in 0.21.0, so the cudagraph estimate is pre-emptively subtracted, but the crash-loop had it forced to 0.
--enforce-eager removes the cudagraph pool and estimate variance; trimming cudagraph_capture_sizes is offered as a middle ground.
The file proposes adding auto_kv_bytes in gpu.py as a sibling to auto_gpu_util, emitting --kv-cache-memory-bytes and a computed gate-only --gpu-memory-utilization.
chat-4b-fp8 currently has _profile: null and a static gpu_util:0.9, which blocks the computed-budget path until populated.
The file includes a concrete load recipe and worked numbers for conversational and worker loadouts.
standouts: Absolute KV budget removes the negative-KV subtraction failure but does not make load order irrelevant because the request_memory gate and profile_run activation peak still need live headroom.
The crash-loop was caused by gpu_util set below weights/total, not by co-resident non-vLLM processes being counted in the budget.
The proposed integration point is a new gpu.py computed function auto_kv_bytes rather than the capability resolver's serve_values enum-to-fragment mechanism.
conventions: Uses research-finding frontmatter with type, register, area, status, coverage, and tags.
Sections are numbered and include a verdict up front.
Claims are tagged with evidentiary status: Observed, Inferred, My-idea, External-prior-art.
Cites specific source file paths and line ranges.
Includes tables of measured boot-log values and a final collected-contradictions section.
concerns: Uses strong adversarial language such as 'FALSE' and 'decisively refutes' toward an unspecified anchor, which may be less neutral than a ledger entry ideally requires.
Mixes observed source traces, inferred ecosystem claims, and design opinions without always separating them in the same sentence.
Some numerical caveats (WSL2 mem_get_info, cudagraph estimate-vs-actual variance) are noted but still used to derive precise recommendations.
Proposes a build-seam change in gpu.py without showing the current implementation of auto_gpu_util or gpu.py:96 override.
Uses a future date (2026-06-30) in coverage and boot logs.
notes: A source-traced research finding arguing that --kv-cache-memory-bytes makes KV sizing deterministic and co-tenant-independent, but startup still requires a computed gpu_util gate and ordered load sequencing, and proposing a new auto_kv_bytes integration in gpu.py.
questions: What is the exact current implementation of auto_gpu_util and read_gpu in gpu.py?
Can the profile_run activation peak be measured independently of the KV shelf?
Where and how is the loadout target shape (conversational vs worker) declared in the build system?
Is VLLM_MEMORY_PROFILER_ESTIMATE_CUDAGRAPHS guaranteed to remain at its default True in the deployed build?
What is the 'anchor' document and where does it live?
What does a 'unit' of 16,384 tokens represent in the loadout table, and how is it used elsewhere?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to test the absolute-budget thesis and produce a verdict and load recipe; the file does exactly that, plus a build-seam proposal and explicit contradictions of prior claims.
apparent_themes: Deterministic memory budgeting vs hand-guessed fractions.
Source-grounded correction of prior mental models.
Computed runtime parameters vs static capability flags.
Measured profiling as the basis for deployment recipes.
intention_signals: The file is trying to establish that an absolute KV byte budget is the right keystone for GPU serving, correct the misconception that it eliminates load-order concerns, and integrate the new flag into the build's gpu.py-based VRAM computation layer.
novelty: It combines a detailed source-line forensic of a crash-loop, an explicit 'contradictions of the anchor' section, and a conceptual reframe of gpu_memory_utilization as a startup gate rather than a sizing lever.
contribution: A source-traced boundary analysis of what --kv-cache-memory-bytes fixes and what it does not, a measured profile basis for chat-4b-fp8, and a concrete proposal for emitting the flag via a new gpu.py computed function alongside a gate-only gpu_util.
summary_for_embedding: A research finding that traces vLLM 0.21.0 source and boot logs to show --kv-cache-memory-bytes pins the KV cache shelf deterministically but does not eliminate the request_memory startup gate or profile_run activation-peak headroom, and proposes a computed load recipe with auto_kv_bytes and a gate-only gpu_util.
intention_for_embedding: Intended to validate and refine the absolute-budget serving model by correcting the assumption that load order becomes irrelevant and by specifying where the new flag should be emitted in the build system.
